data.title: Sigma Lithium: Low Costs Make It Ideal For The Next Lithium Upswing
data.description:
Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML) is a green lithium producer with mines located in Brazil. Its proprietary dense media separation [DMS] technology and tailings-recycle enable industry-leading recoveries and unit costs. Unfortunately, over the past 18 months, there's been a 25% surge in global spodumene supply in 2024, with another 15% forecasted for 2025. As a result, Lithium benchmark concentrate prices have come down from $830 per tonne to $612 per tonne, and carbonate spot prices are roughly 20% lower YTD. This glut pressures near-term revenues, potentially reducing full-year sales by 10%-15% at sustained $9,000 per tonne of carbonate. However, SGML's low cost, optionality to withhold volumes, negotiate floor-price offtakes, and preserve cash until market recovery are good news for long-term bulls.

Lithium Macro Headwinds

Sigma Lithium Corporation is a vertically integrated lithium mining and processing company with a focus on sustainable practices of hard-rock lithium production in Brazil. SGML operates the Grota do Cirilo open-pit pegmatite mine in Minas Gerais, with 107 million tonnes of reserves at 1.4% lithium oxide. The company uses proprietary dense media separation [DMS] to produce the crushed, separated, dried ore that is called spodumene concentrate, upgraded to ore with a 5-6% lithium oxide. I've previously covered SGML in April 2025, where I basically took a bullish stance on the stock.

While I did mention the macro risks, it's unfortunate that the worst-case scenario on that front played out. The ongoing glut in Lithium supply hasn't improved enough. There's still a high likelihood of at least a thin supply surplus for the remainder of 2025, which probably translates into further macro headwinds for SGML. Still, I believe the worst part of those macro headwinds should last until 2025, and may even swing into a slight deficit by 2026. If that's the case, I would argue that 2025 is probably around the "macro bottom" for SGML's underlying commodity prices.

Sigma's Low-Cost Edge in a Down-Market

To give you an idea, over the past eighteen months, the lithium market has transformed, with capacity additions in Australia, China, and South America. The global spodumene supply grew around 25% in 2024, with a further 15% expansion forecast for 2025. This supply has outpaced end-market demand, especially in energy-storage systems. As a result, benchmark Chinese spodumene prices fell from late 2024 to around $620 by May 2025. C2 equivalent refers to spodumene concentrate that grades at 5% lithium oxide.

Similarly, lithium carbonate prices in China have retreated over 20% to about $8,145 per tonne. Still, remember that SGML is one of the world's lowest-cost hard-rock lithium producers. So, while SGML still has positive cash gross margins of 35%, these are undoubtedly harsh headwinds that force it to accept lower market prices in the near term. Also, I wouldn't consider these types of margins in "distressed" territory by any means. And in fact, over the long run, this macro situation could be an opportunity because SGML is low-cost, flexible, and can withhold uncommitted volumes.

Management mentioned they can do this until prices recover modestly, then negotiate prepayments or floor-price offtake agreements using their ESG credentials. Additionally, SGML can schedule and stagger investment spending, like the Plant 2 funding, to match market conditions and cash-flow needs. So, overall, there's still plenty of room for maneuvering, operationally speaking. SGML also noted they met their Q1 2025 production target of 68,300 tonnes of battery-grade lithium concentrate. SGML loaded 29,000 tonnes to International Resource Holdings [IRH], adding to the previous 76,000 tonne total shipment.

With Q1 2025 output sold into the spot or short-term contract market, average realized prices have trended below the company's original budget assumptions. But, fundamentally speaking, the company has delivered on what it can control itself. In fact, my original thesis on SGML was that they'll reach and sustain their 270 kt pa output by Q4 2024. So far, this has been the case. Additionally, their BNDES loan largely de-risks their Phase 2 construction cash needs, and as of their latest update, construction is on schedule. After all, this loan is mostly back-end-loaded starting in 2027, so in the short term, it isn't a major cash drain for SGML.

I also imagined their costs would trend lower, likely below the $660 per ton level. This has also played out as management anticipated, as SGML's Q1 showed their costs are around $622 per ton. Naturally, what hasn't played out is the macro variable, namely, Lithium prices have remained weak. And as you might imagine, this has kept SGML's valuation multiples depressed (more on this later).

Furthermore, SGML's Q1 2025 produced 68.3 ktonnes of battery-grade spodumene concentrate, and 62 ktonnes of that was sold into spot and short-term contract markets. The remaining 6.3 ktonnes were added to inventory. This does show they can stockpile a bit until prices recover. Also, SGML's all-in sustaining cost [AISC] CIF-China of $622 per tonne in Q1 2025 already places them in the lowest quartile of the global hard-rock cost curve. So, in that sense, they seem to be in an ideal spot to weather the storm. But obviously, in the near term, the sustained price declines are going to remain the largest headwind. After all, $9,000 per tonne of carbonate, or $3,200 per tonne of spodumene equivalent, will definitely compress margins and reduce free cash flow.

It's also worth mentioning that it's not all bad news for SGML. Their proprietary DMS technology actually delivers plant-level recoveries are over 70%. Also tailings-recycle loop reclaims ultra-fine material, which helps optimize recoveries even further. SGML is continually improving on this process, which has so far lowered plant-gate costs from $397 per tonne in Q1 2024 to $349 per tonne in Q1 2025. Additionally, SGML's CIF costs, including freight and royalties, fell 17% to $458 per tonne. These reductions stem largely from the proprietary DMS technology and process innovations. In other words, these are not one-off cost cuts but sustainable technology-led improvements, which I expect will pay off whenever Lithium's bull cycle starts again.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Moreover, SGML also reduced its short-term trade-finance debt by 42% between 2Q 2024 ($101 million) and 1Q 2025 ($59 million). This basically drove down interest per tonne from $36 to $17. This leaner financing, coupled with positive cash gross margins of 35%, has trimmed finance expenses by nearly $19 per tonne. So, fundamentally speaking, it seems SGML is actually delivering on the metrics they can control, which has definitely softened the hit from lower realized prices.

Even in the bearish scenario at $9,000 per tonne of lithium carbonate, SGML's ultra-low cost position and disciplined capex phasing should keep it comfortably cash-positive and within its debt covenants. However, if prices slide even further, to say, $8,000 per tonne or below, things would look worse and may force management to cut production. Alternatively, they could accelerate off-take prepayments at locked-in floors or postpone Plant 2 drawdowns to preserve liquidity.

However, I still believe we're probably near the bottom of the Lithium cycle in 2025. After all, factors like China's EV purchase subsidies that incentivize automakers and battery makers to restock lithium should help prop up spot prices. And if my bull case plays out, 2026 should be a much better year for the sector as a whole. Of course, that's a macro factor, and no one can predict the future with certainty. But at this point, I feel the balance of probabilities favors 2026 being a much more positive year for Lithium prices.

Now, from a valuation perspective, SGML currently trades at a $611.9 million market cap. As of Q1 2025, they hold $31.1 million in cash and equivalents, against total debt (net of accrued interest) of $165.3 million. So, I estimate their EV at about $746.1 million. Similarly, their EBIT reached $6.8 million for the quarter despite all the macro negatives.

This is a major positive swing from the quarterly EBIT loss a year ago in Q1 2024 of -$0.3 million. It also suggests an EBIT run rate of approximately $27.2 million at this level, which suggests they should be able to sustain operations at these depressed Lithium prices. Ultimately, Phase 2 construction is a variable SGML control, which I still think will pay off nicely if Lithium bounces going forward. However, Lithium prices will remain the wildcard, and this is their main risk at this point.

Still, I think a good valuation metric for now is their revenue. Seeking Alpha's dashboard on SGML indicates it will generate $395.7 million in annual revenues by 2026, implying a forward EV/S multiple of 1.9. For comparison, their sector's median EV/S multiple is 1.7, so it seems SGML remains in line with the rest of its industry for the time being. In other words, I think SGML's stock appears to be tracking mostly this multiple. So, if we assume that by 2027 SGML's production ramps up quickly as management anticipates, and Lithium prices recover by then, the upside could still be significant at these levels.

Conclusion: A "Macro Buy"

So, in the end, I have to reiterate my "Strong Buy" on SGML because the company itself has delivered on what it promised so far. Unfortunately, the macro factors haven't helped, but the company seems to be in a great position to reap the rewards by the time the next Lithium bull cycle kicks in. Just make sure you size your position accordingly, as Lithium can remain depressed for longer than anyone anticipates. And if the price declines a lot more, then SGML could get into trouble. But for now, that "distressed" scenario seems unlikely since management still has comfortable gross margins and a few options to play before reaching critical levels.

data.title: Consensus Price Forecasts - U.S.-China Trade Pact Lifts Industrial Metals Prices
data.description:
S&P Global Commodity Insights discusses consensus price forecasts for industrial and precious metals, including platinum group metals, amid broader market trends.

A temporary deferral of steep US-China trade tariffs renewed demand optimism across industrial metals markets in May, but the upside was limited by lingering concerns over the impacts of still-evolving trade dynamics on global economic growth prospects. Gold prices retreated from recent highs as trade risks thawed but remained elevated as macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties continued to drive investors' flight to safety. Appetite for safe-haven assets continued to bolster the consensus price outlook for gold in 2025–26, while tariff-induced demand worries and pockets of oversupply weighed on price expectations for industrial metals.

After months of escalation, trade tensions between the US and China partially eased as the world's two largest economies reached an agreement on May 12 to temporarily back down from aggressive "reciprocal" tariffs for a period of 90 days. The US slashed its import duties on Chinese goods from 145% to 30%, while China lowered its tariffs on US products from 125% to 10%. However, the reprieve may prove fragile after both the US and China alleged violations of the agreement just weeks after it entered into effect.

The trade-weighted index of the US dollar found temporary support from the US-China tariff pause but ended the month 10.1% weaker year-to-date. Despite the recent winding down of US-China import levies, the ongoing volatility in the global trade landscape continues to fuel concerns over supply chains, consumption levels and global economic growth prospects. The US Federal Reserve in its May 6–7 meeting said it downgraded US GDP growth expectations for 2025 and 2026, as tariffs stoke rising risks of inflation and unemployment. Global GDP growth outlooks from S&P Global Ratings have also been lowered amid trade uncertainty. Purchasing managers' indexes in the US and China were in contractionary territory in May, signaling a slowdown in economic activity.

China has expanded efforts to stimulate its domestic economy amid mounting trade challenges. The People's Bank of China announced May 6 that it would cut the reserve requirement ratio for banks for the first time since September 2024 and reduce housing loan rates. However, evolving trade dynamics and a sluggish recovery in the domestic property sector continue to drive downside risks for China's economic growth outlook.

After rising above the $3,400 per ounce threshold in late April, the London Bullion Market Association gold price pulled back in May to average $3,277.99/oz. As a temporary pause in US-China bilateral tariffs signaled cooling trade tensions, COMEX-based hedging slowed, and net long positions on COMEX gold declined in May. Yet, with the trade landscape remaining volatile, worries persist over potential inflationary pressures and slower global economic growth. Macroeconomic risks combined with geopolitical turmoil to bolster safe-haven demand for gold. Physically backed exchange-traded fund inflows reached a record high in April amid a surge of inflows into Asian funds, while central bank gold purchases remained robust in March. Consensus target prices for gold have been upgraded 1.5% on average for the 2025–28 period and downgraded 0.1% for 2029.

Bullish fundamentals and US dollar weakness kept the silver price supported on either side of $33/oz in May. Industrial applications in clean energy technologies and electronics fuel demand optimism for silver, while supply levels are expected to remain in deficit for the fifth consecutive year in 2025. A reprieve in trade tensions contributed to upside momentum for the silver price, but gloomy global economic growth prospects limit support. Silver consensus price forecasts have been adjusted 0.6% higher on average across 2025–28 and 0.3% lower for 2029.

The platinum price reached a two-year high of $1,094.60/oz on May 23, supported by expectations for a persistent structural deficit. In its latest quarterly report, the World Platinum Investment Council noted a shortfall of 816,000 oz in the platinum market during the March quarter, the largest single quarterly deficit in six years. Robust investment demand amid tariff-induced uncertainties offset reduced consumption in the automotive and industrial sectors during the quarter. Although overall demand is expected to decline for full-year 2025, supply will remain constrained, keeping the market deficit intact for a third consecutive year. Market tightness is limiting the downside for the platinum consensus price estimates, which have been lowered 0.5% on average for the 2025–29 period. Meanwhile, the palladium price largely moved below $1,000/oz in May, and consensus price targets have been downgraded 1.1% on average over the five-year forecast horizon.

The London Metal Exchange three-month (LME 3M) copper price recovered to reach a monthly peak of $9,610.00 per metric ton on May 23 as relaxed US-China trade barriers signaled renewed opportunities for copper trade. However, slowing manufacturing activity in the US and China amid lingering concerns over potential tariff impacts, a sluggish China property market and elevated refined supply, particularly in the US, limited price support. As anticipation of potential tariffs on US imports of copper encouraged front-loading, shipments jumped 55.5% month over month in March. On May 22, COMEX stocks exceeded levels held at the LME for the first time since March 2022, subsequently narrowing the COMEX-LME cash price arbitrage. Meanwhile, China copper cathode imports fell 8% year-over-year January–April, as domestic refined copper output rose 5.6%. Refined supply is largely expected to remain in a surplus through the end of the decade, while a concentrate deficit is projected to widen through to 2035. A concentrate supply squeeze prevails now, with spot treatment charges (TCs) continuing to tread record low levels. Consensus price forecasts for copper have been downgraded 0.3% on average for 2025–28 and adjusted 3.0% higher for 2029.

The brief respite from US-China trade tensions likewise drove the LME 3M zinc price to a monthly peak of $2,765.00/t May 14, but still-evolving trade dynamics could jeopardize demand prospects. Front-loading efforts triggered by worries over impending tariffs drove US refined zinc imports 28% higher month over month in March. Yet, the cost implications of increased import duties have weighed on US and China manufacturing activity, as well as US consumer confidence. Net investor positions on LME zinc entered negative territory for the first time since March 2024 amid heightened expectations of slowing global economic growth. Regarding supply, support from a steady decline in LME and Shanghai Futures Exchange inventories was offset by an ongoing loosening of the concentrate market as China spot TCs for zinc extended gains in May. Consensus price outlooks for zinc were lowered 0.2% on average for the five-year forecast horizon.

A stubborn supply glut prevailed over short-lived support from easing US-China trade friction at the LME nickel market, where the 3M price averaged at $15,540.40/t in May. Amid a backdrop of a persistently oversupplied global primary nickel market, investor funds remained net short on LME nickel in May. Nickel stocks at the LME held near a four-year high of 200,000 metric tons during the month. LME inventories are expected to remain robust even as the anticipation of potential tariff hikes could encourage metal flow into the US. Meanwhile, exacerbating demand-side pressure from a tenuous tariff pause, the US Congress is deliberating a proposal to revoke incentives introduced by former President Joe Biden for the purchase of electric vehicles. Slowing EV uptake would adversely impact demand for nickel and other battery metals. Consensus price expectations for nickel have been downgraded 0.2% on average for 2025–29.

The LME 3M aluminum price rebounded to $2,528.50/t on May 14 on the heels of the US-China tariff pause, before slipping to $2,444.00/t on May 30 amid lackluster fundamentals. Inventories at the COMEX and SHFE have fallen 54.1% and 24.3%, respectively, in the year-to-date period through May 30. Meanwhile, LME stock levels improved as canceled warrants — representing metal that could be withdrawn from warehouses — shrank significantly. On the demand side, China exports of semi-manufactured products declined 6% year over year in January–April amid reduced operating rates and profit margins among downstream processors, while North American light-vehicle output projections have been lowered due to the impact of tariffs. Slowing global economic growth as increased tariffs materialize would further stifle demand and widen an already anticipated market surplus. Consensus price forecasts for aluminum have been downgraded 0.2% on average for 2025–27.

The Platts IODEX 62% Fe iron ore price was caught between a narrow range of $96-$101 per dry metric ton in May, with a brief rally to the monthly peak of $102.80/dmt on May 14 on the heels of the US-China bilateral tariff cut agreement. Platts is part of S&P Global Commodity Insights. US sector-specific tariffs on imports of steel, aluminum and automotives continue to drag on market sentiments, alongside a persistent steel oversupply in China. Steelmakers in China face increasing pressure to curtail production amid challenging market conditions, which include a sluggish recovery in the domestic property market and rising trade protectionism. Reduced China steel production would pressure iron ore demand, which is already reflected in January–April iron ore imports, dropping 23.5 million metric tons year over year. The expected global seaborne iron ore trade surplus should be less severe over the next few years, as transportation infrastructure build-out challenges in Guinea slow the supply ramp-up from the Simandou greenfield project. As a result, consensus price outlooks for iron ore have been upgraded 0.4% for 2025 but downgraded 0.3% on average for 2026–29.

The Platts-assessed European cobalt metal price held at $16.00 per pound throughout much of May, buoyed by the depreciating US dollar as the market awaits clarity surrounding the export ban implemented by the Democratic Republic of Congo. The DRC government has yet to announce next steps after the export ban that took effect Feb. 22 reached its three-month review timeline in late May, with some observers speculating on a transition to a quota mechanism. Aimed at addressing abundant supplies, the export ban has yet to significantly impact production levels; major producers such as CMOC Group Ltd. (OTCPK:CMCLF) and Glencore PLC (OTCPK:GLCNF) anticipate steady output. However, DRC cobalt exports to China were lower year over year during the March quarter. Concerns have been raised about the possibility of stockpiled material flooding the market should the ban be lifted. Although EV uptake across key markets was strong in April, longer-term demand prospects are at risk amid accumulating trade barriers, such as US auto tariffs and imminent import duties on critical minerals. The cobalt consensus price target is 0.2% lower for 2025 and unchanged for 2026–29.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: The Market Might Exaggerate The Impact Of Remanufacturing
data.description:
Deutsche Bank just downgraded Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (NASDAQ:ISRG) from Hold to Sell, cutting its price target from $515 to $440.

This has been the first downgrade for the company since early 2022, and the first sell rating since 2013. As expected, the market didn't take it well, and Intuitive Surgical's stock reacted with a 10% intraday drop.

The main reason for the change of mind of Deutsche Bank's analyst Imron Zafar is the increasing use of remanufactured tools, which would reduce Intuitive's revenues from replacements and accessories.

I agree that the robotic surgery market is transitioning from a quasi-monopoly run by Intuitive to an oligopoly with higher competition (especially for easier procedures), but the change in Deutsche Bank's target price doesn't come as a surprise. As I stated in my previous coverage of the company, my estimated fair price was $460 per share.

In this article, I will review the impact of the increasing usage of remanufactured tools and whether the expected growth needs to be adjusted based on this information.

Insights From The Experts

Since I have spent most of my career in finance and economics, I haven't had any hands-on experience with a Da Vinci robot. Luckily, I have two surgeon friends who use da Vinci and other robots daily (I wrote about them in my previous coverage).

Two days before Intuitive's downgrade, I met one of them, who is currently working as a urologist at the Hospital Clinic in Barcelona, and got some valuable insights about his recent experience.

Since the hospital he works in is one of the biggest in the city, they not only use da Vinci systems but also smaller and simpler robots for easy procedures.

As patents get released, some companies cut the distance with Intuitive Surgical, and the increasing competition is obvious, but if it's up to the surgeon's choice, he will always choose a da Vinci model.

And that is just because of the quality, ease of use, and reliability.

There are cheaper alternatives, and hospitals use them for specific procedures where they are most cost-effective, but most are still performed with da Vinci.

The issue with cheaper alternatives is that they are limited to specific procedures and have longer procedure times. If you need to have surgeons for longer, and thus, perform fewer procedures, the costs add up.

One of the new robots they are testing in Barcelona is the locally developed Bitrack. My colleague hasn't had good experiences with it, which doesn't surprise me when taking into account that the total investment to develop it has been €21M ($24M). Intuitive Surgical spent $1,145M in 2024 alone.

Based on this information, I don't see any significant threat to Intuitive Surgical's competitive advantage, which is confirmed by the most recent data.

Intuitive Surgical has an ~80% market share, and even if it declines over the next years, the robotic surgery market will continue as an oligopoly led by Intuitive.

Surgeons are reluctant to learn how to use another robot, especially if it doesn't perform better than da Vinci. And considering Intuitive's financial strength and first-mover advantage, I don't see why it should lose its status.

However, the recent downgrade has not been driven by a slowdown in the number of systems installed, but by a threat to its replacement segment that represents 60% of revenues.

Assessing The Impact Of Remanufacturing

Intuitive Surgical's future growth will be mainly driven by the number of procedures performed, since new da Vinci placements only generate 16% of revenues and have lower margins than services and replacements.

Intuitive's parts can be used between 10 to 18 times, and the company estimates revenues of ~$2,200 in instruments per surgery.

Considering the importance of this segment, a substantial percentage of instruments being sold by a third party at a cheaper price would have a serious impact. However, the remanufacturing idea is not new, and the main question is: What percentage of instruments will be remanufactured?

Da Vinci robots use a wide range of accessories such as scissors, hooks, dissectors, graspers, and needles, among others. In total, there are over 70 different instruments that can be used on a da Vinci.

According to Deutsche Bank's report, around 46% of Intuitive's instruments and accessories could be remanufactured, and they estimate that by 2028, remanufactured tools will erode 10 to 15% of U.S. revenues in this segment.

However, remanufacturing tools is not an easy process, and it requires FDA approval. Looking at the data, I am skeptical about remanufacturing having a substantial negative impact from a long-term perspective.

Until recently, there was only one FDA clearance to remanufacture the 8mm Monopolar Curved Scissors for the da Vinci Si. In March 2025, Restore Robotics received its second clearance, and it can now remanufacture monopolar scissors for da Vinci Xi (compatible with da Vinci 5).

While this will affect revenues for sure, I view it as the normal course of the robotic surgery market maturing, and I expect a smaller impact compared to Deutsche Bank's estimates.

First, there is a positive aspect of making some instruments more affordable, which is the decrease in the total cost of running an Intuitive robot. If hospitals perceive that the cost difference between acquiring a da Vinci and a competitor is not significant, they will choose the former.

Also, it is important to note that the most recent Restore Robotics FDA clearance took over 6 months to be granted since the submission (plus the previous research time), and it is just for one instrument.

Intuitive currently has 5 active systems (dV 5, dV Xi, dV SP, dV X, and Ion) with many different instruments that can only be used on a particular system. In the future, Intuitive will keep releasing new models, so remanufacturing companies will need to keep chasing FDA approvals.

While there will be a market for remanufactured instruments, as it provides cost savings to hospitals (we are yet to see how much), it will mainly serve a limited number of instruments for older systems.

Expected Growth And Valuation: Hold

In my previous coverage, where I focused on Intuitive's business model, competitive landscape, management, and the CapEx cycle, I shared my DCF model with an estimated fair value of $460 per share. Since then, following the sharp decline in Intuitive's share price during the first weeks of April, I started a position in the company (between $420 and $460).

To estimate the fair value, I was forecasting revenue growth of around 14-15% and gross margins expanding to 70% (65% during Q1 2025).

I think what we've said in the mid-term is we have the opportunity, we think, on gross margin to get back to 70%. That's our aspiration.

Source: Jamie Samath (Chief Financial Officer), Q3 2024 Earnings Call.

During Q1, revenues grew by 19%. As usual, above my estimates, as I try to leave a margin of error.

Competition and remanufacturing of certain instruments is not something unexpected, especially taking into account that approval times can be roughly estimated based on FDA submissions and Intuitive's competitors' earnings calls.

Although I believe my estimates were already conservative, I am presenting the updated model to account for the 15% impact by 2028 on the U.S. instrument segment and lower gross margins.

In this scenario, the estimated fair price decreases to $410 per share.

However, in my view, the impact of remanufacturing will be lower than 15% by 2028, since it would require remanufacturing companies such as Restore Robotics to get an increasing number of FDA approvals in a relatively short period of time.

Also, the updated model assumes revenue growing between 11.3% and 14.1% going forward, but most research forecasts higher growth rates for the robotic surgery market (~16% CAGR).

Finally, Intuitive could also benefit from a faster-than-expected increase in its installed base if the perceived total cost of procedures decreases due to lower costs for instruments.

All things considered, I estimate that Intuitive Surgical's shares are fairly priced at around $420-$440. At the moment, Intuitive Surgical is a hold.

Risks To The Thesis

Less than 5% of surgeries worldwide are done robotically (and 70% of those in the U.S.), so there is no doubt that the robotic surgery market has room to grow at a fast pace for decades.

But there are some risks to consider, especially taking into account the 60x FCF valuation multiple that leaves little margin for error.

One is a higher-than-expected impact from remanufactured components. Now that some instruments have been approved for remanufacturing, if Restore Robotics manages to get FDA approvals for other accessories in a short period, margins and revenue growth should be adjusted.

The other risk is tariffs, although it is partially factored into the DCF model. Intuitive manufactures 98% of its robotic systems in the U.S., but 80% of its instruments in Mexico.

Although most of the instruments imported from Mexico are not subject to U.S. tariffs, some of the components used in manufacturing the robotic systems are imported from China and other third countries. While it is hard to accurately determine the impact, management expects a 300bp decline in gross margins.

The biggest risk, in my view, is related to Western countries cutting healthcare spending. If Intuitive's largest markets constrain budgets, the competition would become harder to beat if hospitals were forced to buy cheaper systems.

Since the 70s, spending on healthcare has not only grown in nominal terms (inflation) but also as a percentage of GDP.

If Western governments start cutting budgets allocated to healthcare, prioritizing other categories such as defense, the impact on the healthcare industry would be substantial, especially for Intuitive, as it provides a relatively expensive solution compared to some cheaper competitors.

Conclusions

Despite the recent downgrade from Deutsche Bank based on a percentage of tools being remanufactured, my view on the business hasn't changed, and the price target proposed by the bank is in line with my previous estimates.

Making a parallelism with the mobile phone industry, I view Intuitive Surgical as Apple (AAPL) and Da Vinci as the iPhone.

There will be competitors and cheaper alternatives, and some of the broken iPhones will be repaired. But it doesn't mean Apple's business will perform poorly.

Even if cheaper competitors arise, and some older models' tools are reused, da Vinci will be the first choice for surgeons, and Intuitive will remain at the forefront of the robotic surgery industry due to its first-mover advantage and switching costs.

The industry has decades of fast growth ahead, and Intuitive might be where Apple was in 2013. The only issue is valuation.

If the market exaggerates the impact of tools being remanufactured, and the company delivers a couple of weaker-than-expected quarters, Intuitive could present an attractive entry price.

At the moment, my estimated fair price for Intuitive Surgical is around $420-$440 per share, so it is a hold for now. Below $460, I will be increasing my position, and I will consider Intuitive Surgical a strong buy below $400 per share.

data.title: Investing Beyond Benchmarks: The Case For Unconstrained Equities
data.description:
What is unconstrained equity investing?

An unconstrained investing approach gives professional portfolio managers the ability to construct a portfolio free from the prescriptions and dispositions of a market benchmark. The aim is to build a differentiated portfolio of investments with the potential to provide outcomes that are distinct from those offered by the broader market.

Alister Hibbert, Co-Portfolio Manager of the BlackRock Unconstrained Equity Fund (MAEGX), describes benchmarks as an “artificial construct” when it comes to stock selection. His approach to unconstrained investing entails not simply tilting away from a benchmark but starting from “a blank sheet of paper to build the very best portfolio we can based on individual company analysis.”

The opportunity to apply that analysis and parse potential winners and losers is compelling today, given wide market dispersion. The chart below shows the percent of high-growth versus low-growth companies in the MSCI World Index across time, with the current gap large enough to suggest potential for skilled stock picking to strive to generate outperformance.

All stocks are not created equal

Revenue growth dispersion among global equities, 1996-2024

Because relatively few businesses meet the strict criteria that Mr. Hibbert and his team seek, they typically invest in just 20–30 companies with the goal of maintaining their positions across time to fully benefit from what they view as exceptional long-term growth prospects.

“We aim to give our investments the time to compound their returns while avoiding the distraction of short-term opportunism,” he says. The team prioritizes a deep focus on company fundamentals that ignores the daily distractions of benchmark noise and the temptation to trade on near-term news flow.

And in fact, history suggests it is company fundamentals, such as earnings growth and dividends, that account for the lion’s share of return across time. See chart below.

Fundamentals drive long-term return

MSCI World Index sources of return, 1989-2024

Does greater concentration mean greater risk?

A smaller group of holdings does imply that declines in any one can have a larger impact on overall portfolio return. Yet, the inherent nature of an active approach seeks to mitigate this risk by allowing portfolio managers to center their research intensity on what they see as the highest-potential stocks.

In the funds Mr. Hibbert oversees, including the new BlackRock Long-Term U.S. Equity ETF (BELT), his stance is one of a long-term business stakeholder willing to weather short-term hiccups in pursuit of long-term outperformance. “Truly great businesses dominate industries over multiple years, not quarters,” he says.

Yet, the team’s rigorous approach to fundamental research means they will disinvest if the structural outlook for a business and, therefore, the long-term investment thesis deteriorates.

Why now for a high-conviction, unconstrained approach?

In recent decades, new investment vehicles alongside information abundance, rapid processing times and human attention constraints have all contributed to a shortening of investment horizons in public equity markets. In many ways, it defies the age-old wisdom in “time in” versus “timing” the markets ― because despite a broader tendency toward shortening, the long-term power of compounding remains intact. This creates inefficiencies ― and an opportunity for long-term investors to capitalize. Heightened dispersion in the growth outlook for businesses also makes this a favorable environment for fundamental-based stock pickers.

An unconstrained, high-conviction approach like the one Mr. Hibbert describes applies a long-term lens to identify those rare companies that are market leaders today and have the underlying business strength to remain leaders 10 years from now. It’s a differentiated approach in a market increasingly dominated by opportunism and short-termism.

Investors looking to outsource stock selection might favor such a portfolio, or those who are already broadly diversified and seeking a new source of risk and return to complement their broader portfolio.

As useful as benchmarks are, there is great thinking and investment capability to be realized beyond their borders. An unconstrained approach can help unlock that untold potential.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Danaos: Considerably Undervalued And Could Inflect Higher Soon
data.description:
I first wrote about container ship operator Danaos Corporation (NYSE:DAC) with a Buy rating in early 2024, and it's kept pace with the S&P500 on a total return basis since then. I felt it was too undervalued even with a poor outlook for the containership market due to looming oversupply.

But the oversupply hasn't happened yet, partly due to the Red Sea issues, and the container market remains healthy, and has been strengthening lately. Danaos is even cheaper now, having paid for and taken delivery of 7 newbuilds.

I'm not going to dive into the background of the company or what they do (there are plenty of other articles that cover that) and instead focus on key aspects of why I think the company is an outrageous value at this point, and why management doesn't get nearly enough credit for what they've done.

I think it's just a matter of time until shares inflect higher.

Now Versus 2024

Since the run-up to the upper $90's around this time last year, shares have been rangebound in the low-mid $80's, where they are today.

But at this time:

  • Book Value has increased from $162 to $188 (and probably closer to $194 as of this writing).
  • Contracted revenue has increased from $2.5 billion with a duration of 2.9 years, to $3.7 billion with a duration of 3.9 years!

The last bullet is especially important. Danaos has added a significant amount of charter coverage, at excellent rates, since last year. Danaos added $525 million in charter backlog in Q1 alone, and I expect the backlog to continue to increase over the balance of the year, with 2026 and 2027 getting further locked in.

Outlook

Over the past few weeks, the containership charter market has strengthened significantly, which, I believe, will be a supportive backdrop for DAC to extend leases across its fleet.

In fact, I think DAC will increase revenues and earnings all the way through 2028 (with 2027 to 2028 driven by newbuilds). Even without the newbuilds, I expect charter revenue to decline to low-single digits for the existing fleet. While some 2022-era lucrative leases are rolling off, other leases from before than are being renegotiated at higher rates. Danaos provides exception disclosure for every ship in their investor presentation on pages 9-11.

Euroseas Ltd. (ESEA) just announced a $38,000, 36-38 month contract for a 20-year-old, 4250 TEU containership. ZIM Luanda's extension is another strong data point for this class of ship.

DAC's 27 ships at 4253 TEU under-represent the bulk of uncontracted tonnage for 2026 and 2027, and the latest Harper Peterson data shows strong rates for these size ships.

Last year at this time, everyone was convinced leasing rates were set to decline significantly, instead, they improved. While part of this is due to Red Sea crossings remaining low, I believe that much of this is due to favorable supply/demand dynamics in the class of ships that Danaos operates.

True Book Value is closer to $210/share and exceeds the $188 stated value

As if trading at 0.45x book value wasn't cheap enough, the carried book value is materially understated on top of that.

Danaos did two major write-downs/impairments in the last decade, one in 2016 ($415 million) and another in 2018 ($211 million). These were done on a ship-by-ship basis; some were not impaired at all, while others were written down to almost nothing because the company and its auditors concluded: "The undiscounted projected net operating cash flows did not exceed their carrying value."

They concluded that since these particular ships didn't have a current charter, they were worth nothing more than scrap.

These were 6-year-old ships when they wrote them down to scrap value. All 5 of these ships are still in the fleet, with an average charter rate of $32,000/day, or doing $11.7 million a year each in revenue!

As of today, 28 out of DAC's 74 containerships are carried at $12 million or less, and almost every one of them has a 2025 charter where the revenue is exceeding its carried value (and the ones that aren't are close, for example, ZIM Luanda is carried at $12.7 million versus a $30,000/day or $11 million/year current charter. Not to worry, its next charter is at $35,000/day).

DAC's true book value is closer to $210.

Larger Focus On Repurchases

In my last write-up on Danaos, I speculated that share repurchases would slow in Q2-24 and Q3-24 as the company paid for the new ships directly from cash flow, which is what happened. But since then, they've hit the gas pedal on repurchases. In Q4, they repurchased $47.6 million ($37.8 net after SBC, Q4 is when they issue the bulk of their yearly SBC) and another $32.1 net in Q1.

In April, despite the scary backdrop for global trade, the Danaos management team did exactly what good capital allocators do, buying back 264,605 shares at $73.31/share. A touch over 3 million shares traded in those 6 weeks; Danaos bought back nearly 9% of the trading volume.

Danaos ended Q1 with 18.57m shares, and today that number is at 18.3m (or less).

Insiders at Danaos (mostly CEO John Coustas) own 9.62m shares, or 52.6% of the shares, leaving the float at 8.68m shares (and declining). Danaos management bought back 3% of the float in 6 weeks at the beginning of Q2!

Every dollar spent on repurchases at these levels adds over $2 to book value, and Danaos management finally seems to be leaning into them. To me, this is key to shares inflecting higher.

The path to 2028

Danaos currently has $480 million of cash on the balance sheet, along with $770 million in debt. They're practically net debt-free.

Danaos contractual obligations for the 15 new ships on order are as follows.

Danaos produces roughly $600 million in OCF per year. Even with $100 million in repurchases plus another $45 million in dividends in FY25, they should add another $200 million in cash this year. The company has signaled intent to finance a portion of the newbuilds, but they could pay cash for them if needed.

Danaos earned $470 million in net income in the past 12 months. To be conservative, let's model a 10% decline in the current fleet to $425 million. For the 15 new ships, at an average rate of $45,800/day, 70% EBITDA margin, and D&A of $45m/year, adds another ~$125 million in net income. Assuming 16 million outstanding shares by year-end 2027 (and this could be lower), we have $35/share in earnings and roughly $47/share in operating cash flow in 2028.

The stated book value by then will be around $270.

Especially when ships are contracted out years in advance, does a 5x EPS multiple ($175) or a 4x cash flow ($188) multiple seem extreme in this case? Shares would still trade at a large discount to book!

Conclusion

I recently increased DAC from a 3% position to an 11% position. Between the ultra-cheap valuation, future income visibility, and management now showing that they are serious about repurchases and will take advantage of market dislocations, this has become a fat pitch.

The market is fickle and inflections can happen quickly. Investors weren't big on aircraft lessors like AerCap Holdings N.V. (AER) and Air Lease Corporation (AL) until recently, then all of a sudden, both inflected higher for 25%+ gains in a short amount of time. I think the same can happen with containership lessors, and Danaos is the cheapest of the group, and in my opinion, as well as one of the best runs.

Some investors have been wary about the Star Bulk Carriers Corp. (SBLK) purchases, but these were done in conjunction with a huge amount of repurchases, and they're already sitting on a sizeable gain (their cost basis on the latest purchases was $13.50, and SBLK is trading around $17 now). Let's not forget the home run ZIM investment, either.

I think the market is a few quarters away from realizing that earnings and cash flow at Danaos are going higher over the next 3 years regardless of what happens in the spot market, and shares could inflect higher in a short amount of time (and I still think a take-private deal could happen if shares don't move higher!)

data.title: AMD: AI Day Isn't The Reason To Buy, But Server CPU Is (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) held their AI Day on Thursday, and for anyone looking for an “ah-ha” AI moment, this was not the place to get it. It’s safe to say the market wasn’t impressed, and we found the AI GPU announcements to be, well, underwhelming. The stock closed 2% lower for the day, and RSI (relative strength index) was at 68 headed for overbought territory, but took a U-turn to 58 at current levels, although we saw a mini spike on news of the next-gen AI chips with CEO Lisa Su and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman on stage.

What the event did do is prove AMD’s commitment to carve out its own place in AI and break out from the shadows of big brother Nvidia (NVDA), and we see that potential for AMD considering AI is in its very early innings. That doesn't mean we expect AMD to take share from Nvidia, which dominates 90% of the market as of 1Q25. But, we see AMD’s committed efforts getting rewarded with part of what it estimates to be a +$500B AI chip market by 2028. Having said that, AI is not the reason we’re buying rated on AMD and doubling down on that rating after today. Instead, we think what AMD has going for it in the near-term is its server CPU roadmap; that’s what will support the upside and why we are upgrading the stock to strong buy.

What’s New, Su?

Management revealed new details about its next-gen Instinct MI400 series that are scheduled to ship next year; the chips will be assembled into a full server rack codenamed Helios that’ll compete with, Nvidia’s next-gen Vera Rubin NVL144, also coming out next year. Helios will be powered by 72 accelerators, all connected to make up one massive GPU using Ultra Accelerator Link (UALink), which is an alternative interconnect tech to NVLink (the one Nvidia uses in GB200 NVL72, for example). The chart below compares the interconnect tech of each; we think AMD is trying to build on industry-wide appetite for reducing concentration on Nvidia, especially considering that they specify the CPU/GPU is for any while Nvidia's are specific for their own products.

Helios GPUs will be coupled with sixth-gen EPYC Venice CPUs. Meanwhile, Vera Rubin NVL144 will have 72 dual-GPU Rubin SXM modules and two GPUs per package with a custom 88-core CPU. The below chart outlines how the two compare.

Helios claims a 50% increase in memory capacity and bandwidth with 31TBps and 1.4PBps compared to Nvidia. This would all lead to the assumption that MI400 is a direct winner; Altman even advertised on stage with Su, noting:

When you first started telling me about the specs, I was like, there’s no way, that just sounds totally crazy…It’s gonna be an amazing thing.

It is cool, but it’s not a guaranteed winner. In fact, the added bandwidth and memory make for a bulkier product and could impact performance per watt, which AMD didn’t tell us much about. The angle management is playing is price, trying to squeeze Nvidia, which has been the industry go-to, with “aggressive” pricing. Time will tell what pricing can do for AMD in the AI race, considering we still don't know much about the product pricing. However, what we do know from the MI400 series and even the UALink is that AMD is betting on the market being very ready for an alternative to Nvidia, with Su stating:

The future of AI is not going to be built by any one company or in a closed ecosystem. It's going to be shaped by open collaboration across the industry.

AMD is not selling the MI400 yet, which is the product that competes closest with Nvidia's next-gen tech. Instead, what it is shipping (as of last month) is its Instinct MI355X, which should compete with Nvidia’s B100/B200 chips (both of which have already been out since last year). The MI355X is no game changer, in our opinion, considering Blackwell is out and now all eyes are on what's next. Nonetheless, AMD is getting customer traffic from Tesla, xAI, Cohere, Meta Platforms, and even Oracle (ORCL), which is planning to provide its customers with clusters of +131,00 MI355X chips.

FYI: WCCFTech issued a report just this month breaking down the latest market share splits with the PC GPU market. This will become an increasingly important market to track, especially with 86% of desktop PCs estimated to host graphics cards during the next five years, with a market penetration rate of 15% by 2028. The chart below outlines the total PC GPU share as of 1Q25, signaling that AMD needs to bring its AI game. Nvidia is moving fast, stealing share from AMD, with the latter's share growing from 16% to 20% between 4Q24 and 1Q25.

We Like The Server CPU Roadmap

Remember way back in 2017 when AMD still didn't have barely any share within the server market; since then, the company hit 25% share in 2023 and grew that to 33% last year, and in 1Q25 took that number to 39.4%. For the current year, AMD's server CPU share should surpass 40% comfortably and that's on a mix of good execution and lagging competitive edge from Intel (INTC) in the near-term as the latter gets its ducks in order. A report from Digitime references supply chain sources that point to AMD capturing 50% of the market by 2026. We think AMD's real and tangible growth catalyst over the next two quarters is its server CPU roadmap driven by its Ryzen; for Q1, AMD grew its share 6.1% Y/Y and 3.1% sequentially. AMD is doubling down on high-cost performance to expand its share, and it's paying off.

It's also growing on the Client side, too, overtaking share from Intel, which is still trying to find stability under new management with Lip-Bu Tan. In Q1, AMD grew its share by 10.2% Y/Y and 2.7% sequentially to 26.5% and its desktop sales were up 15.2% Y/Y and 6.4% Q/Q.

What To Do With The Stock?

AMD remains on our buy list as we expect it to be an outperformer this year. Since we upgraded to buy in early May, post Q1 earnings, the stock is up roughly 20%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), and we think investors should double down after the AI Day trade down. Valuation remains relatively high with a forward P/E of 63.69x and an EV/Sales of 7x versus a group average of 29.91x and 3x, respectively. But, AMD’s forward P/E and EV/Sales are much more favorable than its TTM P/E, which sat at 88.44x, and its TTM EV/Sales at 6.99x. With that in mind, we think this is one of the cases where investors should overlook the premium multiples because growth is coming to justify them.

The chart above shows that we have been more on the bearish side on AMD since the AI boom began, but we now can say that we believe AMD is starting the slow process of carving out a space for itself within AI. While we still don’t see share gain against Nvidia in the mid-term, we do think the odds are better than they were a year ago and six months ago, and think their position within server CPU is the secret star of the show.

data.title: AI: The Challenges For Investors
data.description:
In part one of our new series, AI Alpha, we explored the sweeping potential of artificial intelligence (AI) as a transformative force. But alongside that opportunity lies a more complicated reality. In part two, we examine the structural challenges, strategic trade-offs, and competitive pressures that we believe will shape how AI ultimately delivers economic payoff. Understanding AI as a general-purpose technology helps clarify both its disruptive power and the obstacles that may delay or dilute returns.

General-Purpose Technologies: Unseen Engines, Uncertain Returns

AI stands as the latest chapter in the storied history of general-purpose technologies, each breakthrough having laid the foundation for a transformative techno-economic revolution.

For example, the printing press revolutionized the mass production of texts, profoundly influencing education, religion, science, and politics; the steam engine powered factories, trains, and mining operations, enabling mass production and transforming transportation; electricity powered industrial machinery, household appliances, and modern communications, reshaping production, consumption, and interaction; and computers and the internet transformed commerce, communication, and innovation by processing and transmitting information. Each of these technologies redefined society by providing versatile platforms for mass production, dissemination, and connectivity.

Today, AI’s general-purpose capability stems from its ability to translate various types of data into numerical representations and compute similarities between them, thereby enabling its application in a wide array of settings. By leveraging vast datasets and sophisticated algorithms to extract hidden patterns and drive decision-making, AI is emerging as a general-purpose technology poised to redefine economic structures, spur innovation across sectors, and reshape societal functions.

Zero ROI

Left to themselves, general-purpose technologies command no tangible return on investment. As Paul David1 elucidates in his analysis of technological transformations, two principles are paramount. First, the evolution of techno-economic regimes built around these technologies often spans decades - returns eventually emerge, though after a long gestation period. Second, general-purpose technologies require a suite of complementary products, infrastructure, training, and organizational changes to realize their full economic value.

This second point underscores the inherent complexity of broad technology adoption. Providers of the technology therefore invest in nurturing complementary innovations, sometimes developing them in-house. Early electric utility companies, such as Edison Electric Company (General Electric’s precursor) and Westinghouse Electric recognized that raw electricity was of little use until harnessed by consumer products that, in turn, drove demand. Today, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang follows this time-tested playbook, understanding that graphics processing units (GPUs) have limited value without complementary software. In AI’s layered ecosystem, GPUs train AI models that must be integrated into further complementary applications.

AI, in itself, offers no immediate return; it is only through the creation of complementary products - be they digital applications, autonomous vehicles, or humanoid robots - that its full potential can be realized. ChatGPT may have served as AI’s light bulb, but as we progress into our Information Age, inventors are still racing to create the toasters, washing machines, and refrigerators of the AI era.

A self-reinforcing flywheel is at work here: each leap in AI spurs inventive responses that, in turn, accelerate further technological advances. I believe the promise of AI will unfold gradually, contingent on the maturation of complementary products and broader societal shifts, including labor reorganization and even political recalibrations in response to changing employment landscapes.

Investors must recognize that the journey toward AI’s trillions of dollars in impact is both intricate and protracted - a transformation unfolding as gradually as the evolution from a solitary light to a fully furnished home complete with all its essential appliances. Yet, this steady progression often clashes with a market that rapidly reprices assets based on shifting narratives - a dynamic tension that poses constant challenges.

Darwin, Fermat, and Pascal: Why AI May Not Lead to Profitability

In 1859, Charles Darwin observed, “It is not the strongest of the species that survives, nor the most intelligent, but the one most responsive to change.”2

And in 1994, another Charles, Mr. Munger, echoed that spirit of navigating complexity with a different lens; he noted:

“The Fermat/Pascal system is dramatically consonant with the way that the world works. And it’s a fundamental truth. So you simply have to have the technique... One of the advantages of a fellow like Buffett, whom I’ve worked with all these years, is that he automatically thinks in terms of decision trees and the elementary math of permutations and combinations.”3

Both point to the same reality: survival and success depend on the ability to assess risk, adapt, and act. And I believe AI’s potential impact must be viewed through that lens.

AI’s potential impact is vast. We are in the Information Age that has been evolving for more than 50 years. The true effect of AI will depend on the businesses that integrate it, the innovators who build its return on investment (ROI) within digital ecosystems, and the managers navigating complex strategic choices with outcomes that may ultimately lie beyond their control.

If a business saves or generates an extra $1 million through AI, the question remains: does that benefit translate directly to its bottom line, or is it passed on to consumers through competitive pressures? Investors who assume every dollar will boost profitability may be disappointed if market dynamics force these gains to accrue elsewhere. AI will likely be a strategic investment rather than an economic investment for many businesses, as they invest in AI not for immediate revenue or cost savings but simply to remain competitive. The decision tree below reflects some of the decision paths facing businesses.

As the chart above illustrates, managers face a branching decision tree when considering AI investments. Where a company has a strong competitive advantage, the arithmetic is straightforward - AI can drive additional revenue and/or improved margins, potentially reinforcing existing advantages and delivering what shareholders anticipate. In these scenarios, firms may benefit from enhanced products and lower costs, allowing them to capture a greater share of value.

In contrast, in more fiercely contested markets, companies with weak competitive advantages lack the luxury of dominance. Here, competitors rapidly anticipate and match improvements, forcing managers to invest in AI merely to stay in the game - even if such investments do not translate directly into increased revenue or profitability. Although AI may reduce total expenses when its cost savings exceed the investment, whether this translates into higher margins depends on the elasticity of demand and the relentless force of competitive pressures. Like rising blinds in poker, every player must commit more resources just to remain competitive, often passing the benefits onto customers rather than the bottom line. I believe most businesses will find themselves trapped in this competitive arena, where substantial AI expenditures, despite their promise, ultimately vanish from the financial statements - a stark reminder that in many competitive environments, survival demands economic sacrifices at the altar of innovation.

These competitive market dynamics reflect the broad magic of capitalism at work - where better products emerge at lower prices and the relentless force of competition curtails profit margins beyond any simple ROI calculus. Just as peacocks incur costs for their elaborate displays or as Costco invests in wages and quality rather than skimming profit, businesses may adopt AI to signal strength, secure market position, or simply ensure survival - regardless of immediate returns.

A new general-purpose technology such as transformer-based AI reconfigures the digital ecosystem, echoing the upheavals Karl Marx highlighted in political and economic ecosystems triggered by inventions such as gunpowder or the printing press. Managers must weigh competition, customer power, and internal dynamics, all while accepting that shareholders might not be at the front of the queue for AI’s spoils.

Game theory problems, as illustrated by the decision-tree managers face - such as prisoner’s dilemma (without cooperation, investment takes place out of self-interest), Pascal’s wager (uncertain downside makes investment the safer bet), or dollar auction (a zero-sum race forces escalating costs) - underscore the short-term rational yet sometimes long-term suboptimal paths companies may follow.

No single forecast or model can capture every nuance of these strategic choices, but prudent investors must build in a margin of safety and weigh broader strategic considerations when making their forecasts. Indeed, the landscape has shifted, and those who fail to adapt, whether business manager or investors, invite their own undoing.

Asymmetries for Public Investors: AI Winners and Losers

The history of investing in technological change reveals an intriguing asymmetry: it is often easier to pick the losers than the winners. As Alasdair Nairn’s Engines that Move the Markets illustrates, when railroads disrupted canals, it was easier to see that canal valuations would collapse than to pinpoint which railroad would ultimately prevail; similarly, the shift from horses to automobiles clearly signaled the obsolescence of equine transport, even if singling out Ford’s Model T as the definitive winner proved challenging. In the dot-com era, while it was difficult to predict Amazon’s rise, it was unmistakable that traditional brick-and-mortar retail would falter, and in the same vein, the transitions from video rental stores to Netflix and from traditional taxi services to Uber reveal that, though identifying the specific victor is challenging, an entire legacy industry could erode away.

This pattern arises because incumbents, built on outdated technological foundations, are not merely competing against a single firm but against a shifting technological frontier that continuously elevates the standard. AI is a general-purpose technology whose impact will ultimately depend on the complementary applications and products that evolve alongside it. Yet, which specific company will prevail as the technology matures and new business models emerge remains a much greater unknown particularly in the digital realm, where the dynamism and complexity of business models is more pronounced than in the physical world.

Success ultimately depends on a multidimensional framework: product-market fit, competitive strength, and the ability to capture value rather than simply pass it to consumers. It is difficult to pinpoint the exact winners in every sector, yet in traditional areas - be it software, transportation, or online advertising - the competitive advantages of incumbent players must be reassessed. In such a competitive and evolving environment, investor success hinges not only on identifying the few winners but, more critically, on evading the far greater number of likely losers.

Today, another asymmetry confronts public market investors with the rise of private capital. Unlike in the dot-com bubble - when companies predominantly raised funds through public markets, leading to volatile share prices - modern firms tend to remain private for longer, favoring venture capital and private equity. As a result, many AI-native companies, which are poised to drive the next technological paradigm, remain out of reach for public investors. While many AI-native startups remain out of reach, public investors who develop a nuanced understanding of how AI reshapes established businesses - by identifying which incumbents possess the culture, management, and competitive edge to potentially leverage these technologies - may still find rewarding opportunities.

Conclusion

Overall, AI’s evolution as a general-purpose technology confirms that true economic transformation unfolds only when innovative complementary products and societal shifts come into play - a process both gradual and inevitable. While the promise of trillions in value drives innovation, the journey is fraught with uncertainty, competitive pressures, and structural shifts that challenge conventional ROI measures; further complicating matters is the intangible nature of AI, which, unlike physical assets, forces investors to rely on faith as much as evidence. As the digital age inexorably marches forward, adaptation is necessary for survival.

________________

1 Paul A. David (1990), The Dynamo and the Computer: An Historical Perspective on the Modern Productivity Paradox.

2 Charles Darwin (1859), On the Origin of Species.

3 Charles T. Munger (1994), “A Lesson on Elementary Worldly Wisdom As It Relates To Investment Management & Business” (talk delivered at USC Business School).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Zhihu: A High-Quality Company Trading At Half Its Cash Balance - With Hidden AI Upside
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Max Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Zhihu Inc. (NYSE:ZH) (HKEX:2390) is China’s leading online Q&A community. The company trades at 50% of its net cash and 65% of its net current asset value (or NCVA, defined as ‘total liabilities’ minus ‘current assets’). Despite becoming profitable in Q4 2024 and maintaining FCF-positive into Q1 2025, the company remains deeply discounted. I believe this is due to two factors: (1) the "China discount," driven by fears of delisting and corporate governance risk, and (2) its status as a microcap with recent progress which has flown under the radar.

Zhihu is executing its two-phase AI strategy. Previously, management used AI to improve operational efficiency and achieve profitability. Now, management is launching new AI-driven products and generating additional revenue streams. I believe the company’s AI progress is underappreciated and could be a major inflection point in the company’s growth trajectory.

Coupled with a management team, who is very committed to shareholders’ returns, I’d argue that Zhihu is one of the most interesting investment opportunities in the Chinese, microcap space today.

Business History: A Company Built for the Long Game

Zhihu is a Q&A platform in China, founded in 2010 by the then 29-year-old entrepreneur Zhou Yuan. Despite his young age at the time, Zhou Yuan had already completed a master’s degree in software engineering, worked for a reputable large company, and founded another tech startup in China before launching Zhihu. His credentials and experiences were only surpassed by his entrepreneurial drive and energy.

Zhihu grew its user base on an invite-only basis during its first two years. Furthermore, the company didn’t begin commercializing until 2017, seven years after its founding. I believe this exceptional discipline was partly thanks to the cheap and abundant capital during China’s Internet boom, which allowed Zhihu to grow without the pressure to generate revenue or profit, and partly thanks to the Founder’s exceptionally long-term vision.

Today, Zhihu is a leading online content community where people come to find solutions, make decisions, seek inspiration and have fun. The company places utmost importance on the quality and trustworthiness of its community members and its content. It believes that in the AI age, the value will accrue to quality, trustworthy content, instead of content which attracts the most eyeballs.

Valuation: Deep Discount

From a valuation standpoint, Zhihu is a screaming Buy. The stock trades at a fraction of its net cash, is free-cash-flow-positive, and has no debt. Yet, the market has largely ignored these facts.

That said, the more interesting question is not whether Zhihu is cheap, but why it has remained so cheap—and whether that is likely to change.

Why Is the Valuation So Depressed?

I believe Zhihu’s deeply discounted valuation is due to two main factors: 1) the “China discount” and 2) Zhihu’s microcap status which means its recent progress is unnoticed by the investment community.

1. China Discount: Delisting Risk and Governance

Delisting Risk

U.S. investors have worried about the potential delisting of Chinese ADRs under the Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act (HFCAA), which was signed by President Trump in 2020. The HFCAA required foreign companies to allow US regulators to inspect their audits or face delisting from the US. The US auditing agency, called the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB), determined at that time that they were unable to inspect the audits of Chinese companies, raising a real threat that Chinese companies would be delisted from US exchanges. However, the PCAOB gained full inspection access in December 2022 and lifted its previous determinations, easing these concerns substantially.

Zhihu has been dual-listed in Hong Kong since April 2022. Even in a worst-case U.S. delisting scenario, investors can maintain exposure via its HK listing. Management’s decision to list in Hong Kong in 2022 shows their commitment to stay publicly listed.

Corporate Governance

Zhihu can easily be disregarded for potential governance problems due to its microcap status and Chinese origin. However, I would argue that the company has exhibited characteristics and behaviors of an extremely shareholder-focused company.

Firstly, the company’s founder, Zhou Yuan has not sold a single share since the IPO. Last year, he didn’t participate in the tender offer, during which the company offered to buy back 15.9% of outstanding shares at an almost 15% premium to the average closing price for the 30 previous trading days. As shown below, his total share ownership has remained the same since 2021. He has only converted some Class B (10-vote) shares into Class A (1-vote) to avoid consolidating excessive voting power.

Secondly, the company has actively reduced its share count post-IPO. Between 2022 and 2024, the company’s share count shrunk by almost 20%.

Note: The increase in share count in 2022 was due to the company’s secondary listing in Hong Kong in April 2022.

Thirdly, Tencent (HKEX:0700) and Kuaishou (HKEX:1024)—two of China’s most credible internet companies—are significant shareholders and have not sold any shares since the IPO. Tencent is probably the most respected Internet company in China, as the creator of WeChat and the largest video game company in the world. Kuaishou is the direct competitor of Douyin (creator of TikTok) in China, and also a well-known, well-respected Internet giant.

2. Microcap with Low Liquidity and Unnoticed Turnaround

Low Daily Trading Volumes

ADS volume is roughly $1.5M/day and HK trading is only ~$170K/day—too illiquid for many institutional funds.

Recent Profitability

Q4 2024 marked the first profitable quarter in both net income and FCF. In Q1 2025, the company continued showing positive non-GAAP Net Income, which is a proxy for Cash Flow generation.

I believe that management could have easily generated positive GAAP Net Income. However, their goal is simply to be cash flow neutral, while investing the excess cash in capitalizing on AI opportunities. Below is what their CFO, Wang Han said during their Q4 2024 and Q1 2025 earnings calls.

"Given our strong cash position, we may choose to maintain a slight overall loss temporarily to capture the potential opportunities arising from deeper integration between Zhihu and AI." CFO Wang Han, Q4 2024 earnings call

“With continued operational refinements and more granular management execution, we are confident our financial performance will exceed our initial expectations for the year.” CFO Wang Han, Q1 2025 earnings call.

Why the Valuation Could Re-rate

Capital Return

With a strong balance sheet and positive FCF, management has ample room to return capital through accelerated buybacks or dividends.

Management has been buying back shares both in the open market and via the tender offer in July 2024. In addition, the company has been buying back shares via its employee trust fund, Plus Channel Limited. In recent quarters, Plus Channel Limited began reporting its purchasing activities because its share ownership has crossed 5%. Based on the 13D filings, we can see that Plus Channel Limited has been very actively buying back shares in the market.

Buying back shares is a very tax-efficient way to return capital to shareholders. Its downside, however, is it reduces the float and liquidity even further. I would not be surprised if management decides to return capital via a special dividend, which will be an immediate boost to shareholders’ returns.

AI Monetization

The platform’s AI strategy is just beginning to bear fruit. After leveraging AI to improve efficiency and achieve profitability, Zhihu has entered Phase 2 of its AI strategy—new revenue opportunities. Management launched Zhihu Zhida, their ChatGPT-style product, as the gateway to access its knowledge library and community in June 2024. By December 2024, Zhida’s active user base had grown to 10M. As of March 2025, the active user base was already reaching 14M and continued growing rapidly, as disclosed in the company's Q1 2025 earnings call.

Zhihu has been launching new features to improve its user experience. The most notable feature recently was “Contributor Attribution.” From the inquiry results, users can trace back to the actual community contributor. Subsequently, users can directly ask/mention the contributor. This is a win-win for both users and contributors – users get the trusted, expert content; contributors get the recognition and corresponding reward.

In the latest earnings call, management has already spoken about marketing/branding initiatives with big brands. For example, the company partnered with Haier (HKEX:6690), Gree, Lloyd, and Baidu, Inc. (BIDU) to create an immersive offline experience with the Zhihu Future Store. The company identified 5 key AI home appliances and AI lifestyle themes, helping brands establish credibility and improve the visibility of their products.

Many of these efforts have not shown up in revenues yet. However, we can see a strong improvement in Zhihu’s user engagement. In Q1 2025, the average daily user time span rose 17.6% YoY to 38 minutes—a strong signal of growing engagement. I believe it is a matter of time before such user engagement will translate to tangible revenues and profitability.

“For our marketing services, it has been evidenced that relatively low quality marketing content conception has dropped to a very low level. And we are now seeing an accelerated momentum in both client mix optimization and commercial product updates. On top of that, AI is unlocking new marketing product opportunities.” CFO Wang Han, Q1 2025 earnings call.

“We believe our marketing services business has reached a strategic inflection point. One's disadvantages and their traditional commercial paradigm is now increasingly transforming into structural advantages in AI era.” CFO Wang Han, Q1 2025 earnings call.

Investment Risks

AI is a new and hotly contested space, which means it is very difficult to forecast how the competitive topology will evolve. I have a strong conviction in management’s focused execution and Zhihu’s high-quality content, however. Zhihu management has begun executing their AI vision seriously since 2022. Their early achievements in higher user engagement and massive growth in Zhida are very encouraging.

Separately, the China discount may not go away anytime soon. The ongoing tariff negotiation between the US and China highlights the fragility of the current geopolitical order, where an existing hegemony is meeting a rising superpower. Some investors may deem Chinese companies uninvestable due to geopolitical risks.

I believe the US and China have fundamental differences in their political systems and cultural beliefs. However, thanks to their economic interdependence and nuclear deterrence, I believe that the US and China will not resort to complete decoupling. The world is watching the leaders of the two countries closely. In a way, the digital revolution of the last few decades has improved international communication and reduced the risk of severe misunderstandings between countries.

Zhihu is a “more trustworthy version of Reddit”

Zhihu is often compared to Quora in the US. However, I believe this comparison is unfair and indeed very unfortunate for Zhihu, given how poorly Quora has fared in the US. Zhihu is a highly innovative and ambitious company. Zhihu has been constantly developing, scaling and refining its business models over the years. One example is Zhihu’s vocational training business, which caters to the education sector. Management announced its entry into the vocational business in 2021. By the end of 2023, the company had already generated 15% of its revenues from this segment. Subsequently, in 2024, Zhihu refined the vocational training segment to focus on the self-operated business which has higher quality and better profitability. This ability to develop, scale, and refine new business models is very impressive, and nothing like Quora.

In a recent interview with CNBC’s Squawk Box Asia, CFO Wang Han described Zhihu as "a more trustworthy version of Reddit, Inc. (RDDT)." In my opinion, Reddit is a better proxy for Zhihu, especially in the AI era.

Conclusion: A Deep Value + Growth Opportunity

Zhihu offers an exceptionally rare combination of a free cash flow-positive company trading at half its cash balance, backed by blue-chip tech giants like Tencent and Kuaishou. With fundamentals already in place and multiple growth catalysts ahead, the downside appears limited—if AI initiatives fall short, management still has ample flexibility to return capital. But if Zhihu’s AI vision plays out, the upside could be extraordinary.

To close, I’d like to share this provocative insight, which has been expounded by both CEO Zhou Yuan and CFO Wang Han on various occasions.

"In the last generation of the Internet, ad dollars followed user traffic. In the next generation, AI-driven platforms will prioritize quality. The most trusted content will win."

And Zhihu is emerging as a front-runner in that shift. According to Qubit Research, AI assistants cite Zhihu more frequently than any other Chinese content platform in professional domains—nearly 4x as often as the next most-cited source!
